title: Menopause & HRT
oneLiner: Physiologic transition with vasomotor, sleep, and urogenital symptoms; HRT provides effective relief but requires individual risk–benefit assessment (NICE NG23).

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Pulmonary embolism (if on oestrogen-containing HRT):** acute dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, unilateral leg swelling, hypoxia (SpO₂ <94%). **Key finding:** Wells’ score ≥6 + raised D-dimer → start LMWH until CTPA.
- **Acute coronary syndrome (atypical in women):** fatigue, nausea, upper back pain, or "just hot flushes" without typical chest tightness. **Key finding:** ischaemic ECG changes (ST depression/T inversion) or elevated high-sensitivity troponin.
- **Stroke (if on HRT + hypertension/migraine with aura):** sudden onset focal weakness, speech disturbance, facial droop. **Key finding:** FAST positive, NIHSS >0.
- **Thyroid storm (mimics severe hot flushes):** tachycardia >130, fever, agitation, exophthalmos. **Key finding:** suppressed TSH + markedly elevated free T4.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Vasomotor: hot flushes, night sweats (often interfere with sleep)
  - Urogenital: vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, recurrent UTIs
  - Psychological: mood swings, anxiety, low libido, brain fog
  - Sleep disturbance (fragmentation, early waking)
  - Joint pains, palpitations (non-cardiac)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Pulse: regular tachycardia (sinus) — but check for atrial fibrillation
  - BP: may be elevated (perimenopausal change)
  - Pelvic exam: atrophic vaginal mucosa, reduced lubrication (if urogenital focus)
  - Body habitus: central weight gain (common in peri/menopause)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Menopause transition** (perimenopausal): irregular cycles, vasomotor symptoms, elevated FSH (>25 IU/L) after 12 months amenorrhoea.
  - **Thyroid dysfunction** (hyper/hypo-): weight change, tremor, goiter, abnormal TSH.
  - **Depression / anxiety** (pseudo-menopause): no hot flushes per se, but overlapping mood/sleep; PHQ-9/GAD-7 scores high.
  - **Cardiac arrhythmia** (paroxysmal atrial fibrillation): palpitations, irregular pulse, ECG with AF.
  - **Chronic fatigue syndrome / fibromyalgia** (non-cyclical fatigue, widespread pain).
- **Splitter:** **Presence of flushes** (vasomotor) – if absent, strongly consider thyroid or psychological cause; if present and cycles irregular, menopause transition is >90% likely.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (exclude AF, ischaemia) if palpitations present
  - Urine dip (UTI – cause of confusion or worsening symptoms)
- **Bloods:**
  - Serum FSH (only if diagnostic uncertainty; if >25 IU/L + amenorrhoea ≥12 months, confirms menopause)
  - TSH, free T4 (rule out thyroid)
  - FBC, U+E, LFT (baseline before HRT)
  - Fasting lipids, HbA1c (cardiovascular risk assessment)
  - If abnormal bleeding: pelvic ultrasound + endometrial biopsy
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: transvaginal ultrasound if abnormal uterine bleeding
  - Second-line: endometrial biopsy if endometrial thickness >4 mm (or >5 mm on HRT)
- **Special tests:**
  - DEXA scan (if osteoporosis risk factors or premature menopause <40 years)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **STRAW+10 staging:** reproductive → menopausal transition → postmenopause; clinical assessment is primary.
  - **NICE definition:** 12 months of amenorrhoea (no other cause) for natural menopause; earlier for surgical/medical.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test (urine or serum)** in any woman of reproductive age with amenorrhoea — rule out gestation before diagnosing menopause.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute PE/ACS/stroke suspected → activate respective protocol (CTPA, troponin, CT head)
  - If symptomatic brady/tachycardia → treat per ALS
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - For menopausal symptom treatment: start HRT after risk assessment
  - For urogenital symptoms: vaginal oestrogen (creams/pessaries) — no systemic progestogen needed
  - If contraindication to HRT / patient declines: non-hormonal options (clonidine, gabapentin, SSRIs – off-label)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **HRT initiation:**
    - Perimenopause (cycles still present): combined HRT (oestrogen + cyclic progestogen) e.g. 2mg oestradiol + 1mg norethisterone 12 days/month
    - Postmenopause (≥1 year since last period): continuous combined HRT e.g. 2mg oestradiol + 1mg norethisterone daily
    - Hysterectomy: oestrogen-only HRT (no progestogen needed)
    - Local vaginal oestrogen for urogenital atrophy only (low systemic absorption)
  - **Risk mitigation:**
    - Screen for VTE risk (personal/family history, obesity, immobility, thrombophilia)
    - Screen for breast cancer risk (woman’s age, family history, BRCA)
    - Check BP, lipids, glucose at baseline and at 3 months
  - **Alternative therapies:**
    - Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) for hot flushes
    - Isoflavones (red clover) – modest benefit; not standard
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):**
  - Warn patient HRT may need adjustment if surgery/immobility planned (consider temporary discontinuation to reduce VTE risk)
  - If on HRT and admitted with acute illness: continue unless contraindicated or VTE event suspected
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard (LMWH + TEDs) if additional risk factors present, irrespective of HRT
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Oestradiol (oral): 1-2 mg daily (e.g., Estradiol 2mg tabs) – continuous for systemic HRT
  - Norethisterone (oral): 1 mg daily for 12 days/month (cyclical) or 1 mg daily (continuous combined) – in combination products
  - Vaginal oestrogen: estradiol vaginal tablet 10-25 µg daily for 2 weeks then twice weekly; or conjugated oestrogen cream 0.5g daily
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Transdermal oestradiol patch: 25-100 µg/24h (e.g., Estradot, Evorel) – less VTE risk than oral
  - Micronised progesterone (for progestogen component): 200 mg oral at night for 12 days/month (e.g., Utrogestan)
  - Tibolone: 2.5 mg daily (synthetic steroid for postmenopausal women – avoids cyclical bleeding)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Oestrogen: DVT/PE history, breast/endometrial cancer, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, severe active liver disease, migraine with aura
  - Progestogen: breast cancer, severe liver disease, uncontrolled hypertension
  - **Important interaction:** Concurrent warfarin – oestrogen can reduce INR; monitor closely.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment needed for mild-moderate renal impairment. In severe hepatic impairment, avoid systemic HRT.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prescribing oestrogen-only HRT to a woman with an intact uterus** → unopposed oestrogen drives endometrial hyperplasia and cancer. Always confirm hysterectomy status and add progestogen if uterus present.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Venous thromboembolism (especially first year of oral HRT – twofold increase; transdermal lower risk)
  - Stroke (increased risk with oral oestrogen, particularly in hypertensive women >60)
- **Chronic:**
  - Breast cancer (small increase with combined HRT, especially >5 years; risk returns to baseline 5 years after stopping)
  - Endometrial cancer (if unopposed oestrogen)
  - Gallstones (oral oestrogen increases risk)
  - Ovarian cancer (small increase with long-term HRT over 5+ years)
- **If not treated:**
  - Osteoporosis, increased fracture risk
  - Persistent sleep disruption, impaired quality of life
  - Urogenital atrophy → recurrent UTIs, dyspareunia, sexual dysfunction
sections.Prognosis: - Menopause symptoms naturally improve over 2–5 years, but urogenital atrophy worsens without treatment. HRT provides rapid relief (days–weeks) for vasomotor symptoms. Long-term benefits include bone density preservation and reduced colorectal cancer risk. Cardiovascular risk profile in early postmenopause may improve with transdermal oestrogen; oral HRT increases VTE risk. Breast cancer risk increase is small (<1% absolute increase with 5 years combined HRT).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check for contraindications before prescribing HRT:** VTE hx, breast cancer, undiagnosed bleeding, hypertension (control BP first).
- **For urogenital atrophy alone, use vaginal oestrogen first-line** — it works locally, requires no progestogen, and has negligible systemic absorption.
- **Start HRT as soon as possible after menopause** (ideally <60 years or within 10 years of menopause) for optimal risk–benefit ratio.
- **Offer a follow-up at 3 months** to assess symptom response, side effects, BP, and tolerability; then annually.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not prescribe oestrogen-only HRT in a woman with an intact uterus** — this is the single most harmful error (endometrial cancer).
- **Do not stop HRT automatically upon admission unless VTE is suspected.** Continue unless surgery planned with ≥4 weeks immobilisation.
- **Do not use HRT as first-line for women with premature ovarian insufficiency <40 years** — this is actually treatment for oestrogen deficiency and carries lower risk than postmenopausal HRT; refer to gynaecology/endocrinology.
- **Do not ignore migraine with aura** — oestrogen HRT can increase stroke risk; consider progestogen-only or non-hormonal options.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden chest pain, dyspnoea, leg swelling (VTE symptoms)
  - New onset severe headache or focal neurological symptoms (stroke/TIA)
  - Unexplained vaginal bleeding after 6 months of HRT – may indicate endometrial pathology
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review at 3 months for symptom check and BP; annual monitoring (mammogram, BP, BMI, symptom review)
  - If on HRT for >5 years, discuss need for continuation with GP/specialist
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / gynaecology registrar if:**
  - New abdominal mass, ascites, or abnormal vaginal bleeding on HRT
  - Failure of HRT to control symptoms after 3 months (consider alternative route/dose)
  - Request for HRT after breast cancer or known thrombophilia
- **Call crash team if:**
  - Suspected PE with haemodynamic instability (NEWS2 ≥7, SBP <90, SpO₂ <90%)
  - Stroke with GCS <12 or seizure
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 → immediate review; ≥7 → call for urgent senior help (anaesthetics/ICU)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L (if concerned about acute illness)

rawMarkdown:
## Menopause & HRT
**One-liner:** Physiologic transition with vasomotor, sleep, and urogenital symptoms; HRT provides effective relief but requires individual risk–benefit assessment (NICE NG23).

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Pulmonary embolism (if on oestrogen-containing HRT):** acute dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, unilateral leg swelling, hypoxia (SpO₂ <94%). **Key finding:** Wells’ score ≥6 + raised D-dimer → start LMWH until CTPA.
- **Acute coronary syndrome (atypical in women):** fatigue, nausea, upper back pain, or "just hot flushes" without typical chest tightness. **Key finding:** ischaemic ECG changes (ST depression/T inversion) or elevated high-sensitivity troponin.
- **Stroke (if on HRT + hypertension/migraine with aura):** sudden onset focal weakness, speech disturbance, facial droop. **Key finding:** FAST positive, NIHSS >0.
- **Thyroid storm (mimics severe hot flushes):** tachycardia >130, fever, agitation, exophthalmos. **Key finding:** suppressed TSH + markedly elevated free T4.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Vasomotor: hot flushes, night sweats (often interfere with sleep)
  - Urogenital: vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, recurrent UTIs
  - Psychological: mood swings, anxiety, low libido, brain fog
  - Sleep disturbance (fragmentation, early waking)
  - Joint pains, palpitations (non-cardiac)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Pulse: regular tachycardia (sinus) — but check for atrial fibrillation
  - BP: may be elevated (perimenopausal change)
  - Pelvic exam: atrophic vaginal mucosa, reduced lubrication (if urogenital focus)
  - Body habitus: central weight gain (common in peri/menopause)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Menopause transition** (perimenopausal): irregular cycles, vasomotor symptoms, elevated FSH (>25 IU/L) after 12 months amenorrhoea.
  - **Thyroid dysfunction** (hyper/hypo-): weight change, tremor, goiter, abnormal TSH.
  - **Depression / anxiety** (pseudo-menopause): no hot flushes per se, but overlapping mood/sleep; PHQ-9/GAD-7 scores high.
  - **Cardiac arrhythmia** (paroxysmal atrial fibrillation): palpitations, irregular pulse, ECG with AF.
  - **Chronic fatigue syndrome / fibromyalgia** (non-cyclical fatigue, widespread pain).
- **Splitter:** **Presence of flushes** (vasomotor) – if absent, strongly consider thyroid or psychological cause; if present and cycles irregular, menopause transition is >90% likely.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (exclude AF, ischaemia) if palpitations present
  - Urine dip (UTI – cause of confusion or worsening symptoms)
- **Bloods:**
  - Serum FSH (only if diagnostic uncertainty; if >25 IU/L + amenorrhoea ≥12 months, confirms menopause)
  - TSH, free T4 (rule out thyroid)
  - FBC, U+E, LFT (baseline before HRT)
  - Fasting lipids, HbA1c (cardiovascular risk assessment)
  - If abnormal bleeding: pelvic ultrasound + endometrial biopsy
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: transvaginal ultrasound if abnormal uterine bleeding
  - Second-line: endometrial biopsy if endometrial thickness >4 mm (or >5 mm on HRT)
- **Special tests:**
  - DEXA scan (if osteoporosis risk factors or premature menopause <40 years)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **STRAW+10 staging:** reproductive → menopausal transition → postmenopause; clinical assessment is primary.
  - **NICE definition:** 12 months of amenorrhoea (no other cause) for natural menopause; earlier for surgical/medical.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test (urine or serum)** in any woman of reproductive age with amenorrhoea — rule out gestation before diagnosing menopause.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute PE/ACS/stroke suspected → activate respective protocol (CTPA, troponin, CT head)
  - If symptomatic brady/tachycardia → treat per ALS
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - For menopausal symptom treatment: start HRT after risk assessment
  - For urogenital symptoms: vaginal oestrogen (creams/pessaries) — no systemic progestogen needed
  - If contraindication to HRT / patient declines: non-hormonal options (clonidine, gabapentin, SSRIs – off-label)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **HRT initiation:**
    - Perimenopause (cycles still present): combined HRT (oestrogen + cyclic progestogen) e.g. 2mg oestradiol + 1mg norethisterone 12 days/month
    - Postmenopause (≥1 year since last period): continuous combined HRT e.g. 2mg oestradiol + 1mg norethisterone daily
    - Hysterectomy: oestrogen-only HRT (no progestogen needed)
    - Local vaginal oestrogen for urogenital atrophy only (low systemic absorption)
  - **Risk mitigation:**
    - Screen for VTE risk (personal/family history, obesity, immobility, thrombophilia)
    - Screen for breast cancer risk (woman’s age, family history, BRCA)
    - Check BP, lipids, glucose at baseline and at 3 months
  - **Alternative therapies:**
    - Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) for hot flushes
    - Isoflavones (red clover) – modest benefit; not standard
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):**
  - Warn patient HRT may need adjustment if surgery/immobility planned (consider temporary discontinuation to reduce VTE risk)
  - If on HRT and admitted with acute illness: continue unless contraindicated or VTE event suspected
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard (LMWH + TEDs) if additional risk factors present, irrespective of HRT

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Oestradiol (oral): 1-2 mg daily (e.g., Estradiol 2mg tabs) – continuous for systemic HRT
  - Norethisterone (oral): 1 mg daily for 12 days/month (cyclical) or 1 mg daily (continuous combined) – in combination products
  - Vaginal oestrogen: estradiol vaginal tablet 10-25 µg daily for 2 weeks then twice weekly; or conjugated oestrogen cream 0.5g daily
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Transdermal oestradiol patch: 25-100 µg/24h (e.g., Estradot, Evorel) – less VTE risk than oral
  - Micronised progesterone (for progestogen component): 200 mg oral at night for 12 days/month (e.g., Utrogestan)
  - Tibolone: 2.5 mg daily (synthetic steroid for postmenopausal women – avoids cyclical bleeding)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Oestrogen: DVT/PE history, breast/endometrial cancer, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, severe active liver disease, migraine with aura
  - Progestogen: breast cancer, severe liver disease, uncontrolled hypertension
  - **Important interaction:** Concurrent warfarin – oestrogen can reduce INR; monitor closely.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment needed for mild-moderate renal impairment. In severe hepatic impairment, avoid systemic HRT.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prescribing oestrogen-only HRT to a woman with an intact uterus** → unopposed oestrogen drives endometrial hyperplasia and cancer. Always confirm hysterectomy status and add progestogen if uterus present.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Venous thromboembolism (especially first year of oral HRT – twofold increase; transdermal lower risk)
  - Stroke (increased risk with oral oestrogen, particularly in hypertensive women >60)
- **Chronic:**
  - Breast cancer (small increase with combined HRT, especially >5 years; risk returns to baseline 5 years after stopping)
  - Endometrial cancer (if unopposed oestrogen)
  - Gallstones (oral oestrogen increases risk)
  - Ovarian cancer (small increase with long-term HRT over 5+ years)
- **If not treated:**
  - Osteoporosis, increased fracture risk
  - Persistent sleep disruption, impaired quality of life
  - Urogenital atrophy → recurrent UTIs, dyspareunia, sexual dysfunction

### Prognosis
- Menopause symptoms naturally improve over 2–5 years, but urogenital atrophy worsens without treatment. HRT provides rapid relief (days–weeks) for vasomotor symptoms. Long-term benefits include bone density preservation and reduced colorectal cancer risk. Cardiovascular risk profile in early postmenopause may improve with transdermal oestrogen; oral HRT increases VTE risk. Breast cancer risk increase is small (<1% absolute increase with 5 years combined HRT).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check for contraindications before prescribing HRT:** VTE hx, breast cancer, undiagnosed bleeding, hypertension (control BP first).
- **For urogenital atrophy alone, use vaginal oestrogen first-line** — it works locally, requires no progestogen, and has negligible systemic absorption.
- **Start HRT as soon as possible after menopause** (ideally <60 years or within 10 years of menopause) for optimal risk–benefit ratio.
- **Offer a follow-up at 3 months** to assess symptom response, side effects, BP, and tolerability; then annually.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not prescribe oestrogen-only HRT in a woman with an intact uterus** — this is the single most harmful error (endometrial cancer).
- **Do not stop HRT automatically upon admission unless VTE is suspected.** Continue unless surgery planned with ≥4 weeks immobilisation.
- **Do not use HRT as first-line for women with premature ovarian insufficiency <40 years** — this is actually treatment for oestrogen deficiency and carries lower risk than postmenopausal HRT; refer to gynaecology/endocrinology.
- **Do not ignore migraine with aura** — oestrogen HRT can increase stroke risk; consider progestogen-only or non-hormonal options.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden chest pain, dyspnoea, leg swelling (VTE symptoms)
  - New onset severe headache or focal neurological symptoms (stroke/TIA)
  - Unexplained vaginal bleeding after 6 months of HRT – may indicate endometrial pathology
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review at 3 months for symptom check and BP; annual monitoring (mammogram, BP, BMI, symptom review)
  - If on HRT for >5 years, discuss need for continuation with GP/specialist

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / gynaecology registrar if:**
  - New abdominal mass, ascites, or abnormal vaginal bleeding on HRT
  - Failure of HRT to control symptoms after 3 months (consider alternative route/dose)
  - Request for HRT after breast cancer or known thrombophilia
- **Call crash team if:**
  - Suspected PE with haemodynamic instability (NEWS2 ≥7, SBP <90, SpO₂ <90%)
  - Stroke with GCS <12 or seizure
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 → immediate review; ≥7 → call for urgent senior help (anaesthetics/ICU)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L (if concerned about acute illness)
